hypotheses.text: Baseline eye-tracking features (saccadic latency variability, fixation stability) that discriminate ADHD from controls in screening studies will predict individual differences in treatment response to VR-based eye-tracking training, such that children with more pronounced baseline oculomotor abnormalities will show greater post-training improvement in attentional control (measured by CPT performance and teacher ratings).
hypotheses.rationale: The knowledge gap (gap_001) identifies that no study connects discriminative eye-tracking biomarkers with treatment response prediction, despite assoc_003 showing the translational bridge from screening to intervention. Using first-principles reasoning from predictive biomarker literature in psychiatry, it is plausible that the same features that distinguish diagnostic groups also index neural circuit malleability and thus predict training outcomes.
hypotheses.required_n: n=120 (60 VR training, 60 active control) with baseline and post-intervention eye-tracking and CPT assessment, 80% power for medium effect (d=0.5)

hypotheses.text: Eye-tracking biomarkers validated in pediatric ADHD populations will generalize to adult ADHD with reduced discriminative accuracy (estimated 10–15% AUC reduction), and different oculomotor features (e.g., anticipatory saccades vs. fixation stability) will emerge as more discriminative in adults due to developmental changes in fronto-striatal circuit maturation.
hypotheses.rationale: The population gap (gap_002) highlights the complete absence of adult ADHD eye-tracking research. Since fronto-striatal circuits implicated in oculomotor control continue maturing into early adulthood, pediatric-derived biomarkers may not directly transfer. This hypothesis operationalizes a cross-population generalization test with a developmental moderation prediction, using Bayesian reasoning to set prior expectations based on known neurodevelopmental trajectories.
hypotheses.required_n: n=200 (100 adult ADHD, 100 adult controls), ages 18–45, using the same tablet-based eye-tracking paradigm as Chen 2023

hypotheses.text: Combining structured CPT eye-tracking metrics (from assoc_001/assoc_002) with naturalistic reading eye-tracking metrics (from assoc_002) into a multi-context oculomotor assessment battery will improve ADHD classification accuracy by ≥8% over any single-context measurement, because task-general and context-specific attentional deficits provide additive diagnostic information.
hypotheses.rationale: The supplement relationship (assoc_002) between CPT eye-tracking and reading eye-tracking suggests these capture distinct but complementary aspects of attentional dysfunction. No existing study combines both contexts. This hypothesis uses a Bayesian integration framework predicting that the conditional independence of these measurement contexts yields additive diagnostic value, a novel approach in the field.
hypotheses.required_n: n=250 (125 ADHD, 125 controls) completing both CPT and reading eye-tracking sessions, counterbalanced
